commit.message:
fix: Track discovered nodes to avoid "rediscovery" (#168)

This violated the contract of `iteration` which says that the `nodes`
passed in are *new* (not already received). In turn, this was breaking
some of the candidate tracking in MMR since duplicates were arising.

This closes #167.

verification.payload:
tree 7d69493c4346ad8cc8676651befc4c1933538bd0
parent a936fa0648a27fb285363913bce531f3e1e45a9b
author Ben Chambers <35960+bjchambers@users.noreply.github.com> 1742810008 -0700
committer GitHub <noreply@github.com> 1742810008 +0100

fix: Track discovered nodes to avoid "rediscovery" (#168)

This violated the contract of `iteration` which says that the `nodes`
passed in are *new* (not already received). In turn, this was breaking
some of the candidate tracking in MMR since duplicates were arising.

This closes #167.

commit.message:
Support dicts in lists in metadata (#148)

* Support dicts in lists in metadata

This allows cases like:

```
metadata = {
    "entities": [
        {"entity": "Bell", "type": "PEOPLE"},
        ...
    ]
}
```

Note: For stores that perform shredding this works by JSON encoding the
entire item `{"entity": "Bell", "type": "PEOPLE"}` into the key. This
means that equality on the items of `entities` are supported, by digging
into fields won't be.

* lint/fmt

* lint

verification.payload:
tree 68e717303063da97a447b9bb25e1d5fd85b05efb
parent b99bddceaf126c3ac682b0c412e8e3b00951ba87
author Ben Chambers <35960+bjchambers@users.noreply.github.com> 1740007153 -0800
committer GitHub <noreply@github.com> 1740007153 -0800

Support dicts in lists in metadata (#148)

* Support dicts in lists in metadata

This allows cases like:

```
metadata = {
    "entities": [
        {"entity": "Bell", "type": "PEOPLE"},
        ...
    ]
}
```

Note: For stores that perform shredding this works by JSON encoding the
entire item `{"entity": "Bell", "type": "PEOPLE"}` into the key. This
means that equality on the items of `entities` are supported, by digging
into fields won't be.

* lint/fmt

* lint

Does *not* use `--strict` when deploying docs

allow local iteration with warnings.

commit.message:
limit get_adjacent to k items regardless of edges (#104)

* get_adjacent returns up to `k` items

This closes #74. `get_adjacent` and `aget_adjacent`
always return up to `k` items.

This requires the implementations to rerank items
returned from multiple queries to produce at most
`k` items.

This also closes #101.

* fixes

* lint

commit.message:
update notebook example (#82)

* update notebook example

1. Added retry to Astra adapter. Deep traversal issuing many calls were
   experiencing an amplification of timeouts due to the number of calls.
   Adding a retry within the traversal alleviated this.
2. Added a `spacy` document transformer which uses Spacy for NER. This
   suffered fewer errors than the previous GliNER implementation on
   large documents.
3. Updated the notebook code to work.

* lint

* update actions?

* spacy

* remove extra installation

* ignore mypy

* ignore

verification.payload:
tree 5b254ff346214ff6cde3b8ec97e1f2479b5833c1
parent 98c08367364bcac9eecb8de5527404c92a18546f
author Ben Chambers <35960+bjchambers@users.noreply.github.com> 1738184994 -0800
committer GitHub <noreply@github.com> 1738184994 -0800

update notebook example (#82)

* update notebook example

1. Added retry to Astra adapter. Deep traversal issuing many calls were
   experiencing an amplification of timeouts due to the number of calls.
   Adding a retry within the traversal alleviated this.
2. Added a `spacy` document transformer which uses Spacy for NER. This
   suffered fewer errors than the previous GliNER implementation on
   large documents.
3. Updated the notebook code to work.

* lint

* update actions?

* spacy

* remove extra installation

* ignore mypy

* ignore

commit.message:
flatten tests (#80)

* flatten tests

The "integration tests" are easily run against in-memory stores and
generally all tests are being run all the time. Flattening them makes
it easier to run all the tests with a specific filter without needing
to know which kind of tests it is using the `-k` option.

* lint

verification.payload:
tree a4f12659fb63d0e088b8671bc26d5254fd8893a3
parent 6ae08de1a33946087bd27d14baa81ab3aaeb6766
author Ben Chambers <35960+bjchambers@users.noreply.github.com> 1738169980 -0800
committer GitHub <noreply@github.com> 1738169980 -0800

commit.message:
remove `str` edge shorthand (#76)

* remove `str` edge shorthand

It is clearer to always specify the edges as `(source, target)`. It also
avoids `[source, target]` being valid and accidentally used instead of
`[(source, target)]`.

* fix tests

* lint

* docs

releases.name: v0.8.0
releases.body:
## What's Changed
* upgraded astra packages by @epinzur in https://github.com/datastax/graph-rag/pull/173
* simplified astra adapter implementation by @epinzur in https://github.com/datastax/graph-rag/pull/174


**Full Changelog**: https://github.com/datastax/graph-rag/compare/v0.7.0...v0.8.0

releases.name: v0.7.0
releases.body:
## What's Changed
* updated adapter tests to ensure documents are returned in similarity order by @epinzur in https://github.com/datastax/graph-rag/pull/172


**Full Changelog**: https://github.com/datastax/graph-rag/compare/v0.6.3...v0.7.0

releases.name: v0.6.3
releases.body:
## What's Changed
* fix: Track discovered nodes to avoid "rediscovery" by @bjchambers in https://github.com/datastax/graph-rag/pull/168
* updated iteration documentation by @epinzur in https://github.com/datastax/graph-rag/pull/169


**Full Changelog**: https://github.com/datastax/graph-rag/compare/v0.6.2...v0.6.3

readme_html:
Graph RAG

Retrievers providing both unstructured (similarity-search on vectors) and structured (traversal of metadata properties).

About The Project

Graph RAG provides retrievers combining vector-search (for unstructured similarity) and traversal (for structured relationships in metadata). These retrievers are implemented using the metadata search functionality of existing vector stores, allowing you to traverse your existing vector store!

The core library (graph-retriever) supports can be used in generic Python applications, while langchain-graph-retriever provides langchain-specific functionality.

Getting Started with LangChain

  1. Install langchain-graph-retriever (or add to your Python dependencies).

    pip install langchain-graph-retriever

  2. Wrap your existing vector store to enable graph retrieval:

    from langchain_graph_retriever import GraphRetriever

retriever = GraphRetriever(
    # Adapt AstraDBVectorStore for use with Graph Retrievers.
    # Exposes functionality of the underlying store that is otherwise not available.
    store = store,
    # Define the relationships to navigate:
    #   1. From nodes with a list of `mentions` to the nodes with the corresponding `ids`.
    #   2. From nodes with a list of related `entities` to other nodes with the same entities.
    edges = [("mentions", "id"), "entities"],
)

retriever.invoke("where is Santa Clara?")

Roadmap

Graph RAG is under active development. This is an overview of our current roadmap - please 👍 issues that are important to you. Don't see a feature that would be helpful for your application - create a feature request!

  • Support more vector stores
  • Support Lazy Graph RAG on the retrieved documents.

Contributing

Contributions are what make the open source community such an amazing place to learn, inspire, and create. Any contributions you make are greatly appreciated.

If you have a suggestion that would make this better, please fork the repo and create a pull request. You can also simply open an issue with the tag "enhancement". Don't forget to give the project a star! Thanks again!

  1. Fork the Project
  2. Create your Feature Branch (git checkout -b feature/AmazingFeature)
  3. Commit your Changes (git commit -m 'Add some AmazingFeature')
  4. Push to the Branch (git push origin feature/AmazingFeature)
  5. Open a Pull Request

See CONTRIBUTING.md for more information on development.

License
